text: Call notes between @asjwebley and @Croesus_BTC * All interpretations are my own. Key developments January marked Smarter Web’s most eventful month since the summer; LSE uplisting news, purchase of 10 BTC, the appointment of a new NED, "Bitcoin Unconference announced" and Michael Saylor confirming no plans to enter the UK. Completion of the restricted uplisting period has enabled more open communication, with Andrew noting it felt like “a weight had been lifted.” Market sentiment and engagement Sentiment has improved following a challenging period for both shareholders and the team. Jesse noted the prior low of ~0.69x mNAV coincided with tax-loss harvesting, pressure which has since abated. A strong start to 2026 (~+50% share price) has driven renewed focus on the core treasury value proposition. Encouraging increase in inbound calls and meeting requests from UK and international investors, indicating growing excitement and support. Market cycles and capital strategy execution Bitcoin treasury companies are inherently sensitive to market sentiment, particularly when trading below 1x mNAV. Regulatory constraints during the uplisting limited certain actions, but management is confident it can deliver shareholder value in future sub-1x mNAV environments - team learned a lot during this period. Smarter Web has options across all market conditions, supported by a growing balance sheet and a flexible toolkit. Liquidity remains a key differentiator, enabling execution via the ATM facility which can scale once on the LSE. Continued demand from both retail and institutional investors (including ranking among the most bought stocks by retail). Management emphasised disciplined execution and long term value creation over unnecessary risk taking. Capital raises and transparency ATM capital raises can temporarily dilute Bitcoin yield prior to deployment (number of shares go up, cash is raised but not deployed) Team emphasised full transparency and believes recent LSE uplisting costs have already been value accretive, as reflected in the share price response. Governance and scaling Andrew acknowledged criticism for being “cautious” but reiterated that long-term success and regulatory credibility take priority. Prospectus approval was attributed in part to running the business in a way supported by regulators and advisers. Larger peers using preferred structures benefit from greater scale, reinforcing the focus on growing Smarter Web 2–4x over time. UK capital markets opportunity Jesse estimated the UK asset landscape at ~£46tn, with disproportionately large equities and money market allocations. Significant capital sits in equities, bonds, and money markets earning modest returns (~4–5%), highlighting demand for differentiated return profiles. The UK commands outsized global capital relative to its size, supporting the view that it is the second largest global capital market by international capital activity (i.e. global investment flows, international asset ownership, and capital managed from the UK but invested worldwide). Bitcoin powered fixed income style opportunity Strategy has stated it has no plans to launch in the UK, without providing a reason. This creates a clear long-term opportunity, as there is currently no listed instruments offering fixed income style returns backed by Bitcoin. While this is further down SWC’s roadmap, the team is highly aware of this strategic opportunity. Index inclusion and passive flows Strong enthusiasm around LSE listing and the passive flows that come, starting with FTSE SmallCap. Progression to the FTSE 250 and beyond would unlock significant passive and institutional demand. The UK has ~£3tn in listed equities, with substantially more in total equity capital when overseas holdings are included. Flows from active managers (not passive) will flow to where performance is Volatility Volatility is seen as a core feature of the Bitcoin treasury model, enabling opportunity rather than representing traditional downside risk. Smarter Web has ~110% realized volatility (time frame wasn't specific but 50 day is ~100% as per the dash board) vs ~30% for Bitcoin, which Jesse characterises as “good volatility” aligned with Bitcoin’s asymmetric upside. Unlike traditional equities, where volatility is typically negative, Bitcoin driven volatility benefits from fat-tailed positive return days, making higher volatility attractive. This profile, combined with LSE listing and removed stamp tax, positions Smarter Web as an equity with a unique profile for traders. Note from the author: UK markets tend to be more defensive in nature, with lower beta and volatility. As a result, there is strong demand for stocks with higher volatility profiles to generate alpha. This attracts a broader mix of institutional investors, including short-term, momentum, and relative value strategies - which in turn supports deeper liquidity and trading activity. Bitcoin outlook and performance Team believes Bitcoin likely experienced a shallow bear phase in 2025, with much of the downside absorbed by treasury companies (specifically Strategy). Andrew has a constructive Bitcoin outlook for 2026 and beyond, supported by fixed supply dynamics and macro conditions. Despite a flat/down Bitcoin year, Smarter Web grew holdings to ~2,500 BTC so team is excited by what they can be in a strong Bitcoin year. Conference and thought leadership Smarter Web is hosting the UK’s first Bitcoin treasury focused conference on May 29th in Bristol. The event brings together UK and global leaders to shape the narrative, share best practice, and accelerate institutional understanding. Reinforces Smarter Web’s role as a thought leader in the UK (and Global) Bitcoin treasury space.

text: Call notes between @asjwebley and @Anders_ * All interpretations are my own Market sentiment & mNAV * Anders increased his position around 0.8x mNAV. * mNAV now ~1.2x as sector sentiment improves alongside various positive catalysts for the company. * Anders said his SWC union jack/bitcoin t-shirt is now his favourite within his collection. * Andrew sees mNAV moving in waves, driven by sentiment and capital-raising ability. * SWC is entering a more constructive phase, with higher mNAV levels becoming increasingly exciting. LSE listing & regulatory progress *Andrew emphasised pride in the LSE listing process and the FCA-approved prospectus, noting that many industry-specific terms were accepted, a positive signal for the broader sector. *Listing costs were low relative to peers. * The team is pleased to now have greater freedom to communicate, following a long restricted period. Strategy & delivery * Andrew reiterated his strong confidence in the company’s short, medium, and long-term strategy. * Hopes the market can see that he's set up the business to weather and benefit from different parts of the cycle. * Important to remember that decisions are based on medium to long term value creation (as part of the 10 year plan), not just short term optimisation. Capital markets & index inclusion * Since IPO, SWC has demonstrated it can raise capital (my estimate is ~£215m across ABBs, ATM, and converts) * LSE access opens the door to larger active and passive capital pools. * Strong preference for equity over debt for future capital raises. * Belief the business could grow 3–4x under the current strategy. * Debt is not preferred, though not ruled out long term. * UK equities market opportunity analysis has reinforced optimism. * Possible there could be potential inclusion in FTSE Small Cap and FTSE All-Share by end of March. * If momentum continues, possible FTSE 250 inclusion around April, coinciding with the company’s first year as a public entity. * Subscription agreement (ATM) viewed as extremely scalable, especially once on LSE. * SWC ranked as the #10 most bought stock by retail (source Winterflood). * Worth mentioning that SWC very likely will be more available via different platforms once listed on LSE, a point previously mentioned on X. Bitcoin outlook & positioning * Andrew believes the traditional four-year Bitcoin cycle is diminishing, driven by institutional adoption (e.g., MSTR). * Surprised Bitcoin hasn’t kept pace with gold and silver. * Views Bitcoin’s underperformance in 2025 as a positive psychological setup for 2026. * Proud of Bitcoin-per-share growth, despite a challenging year for Bitcoin overall. * Optimistic about Bitcoin-related innovation benefiting both Bitcoin and UK capital markets. Costs, balance sheet & risk management * Rising costs acknowledged as a result of hiring high-quality talent. * Andrew spent a lot of time thinking of why SWC traded down to ~0.7x mNAV at the local lows: 1) General sell pressure / weak market sentiment (Anders noted the impact of tax harvesting) and 2) possible concerns around cash runway and could that lead to potential Bitcoin sales. * Andrew has had internal discussions about establishing a GBP reserve / cash buffer to cover a defined operating runway that will give the market more certainty over longevity. * No current plans for proof of reserves, but remains open-minded; reminder that SWC is on the LSE and audited, both which require a high level of transparency. Operating Business * As an operating business, management remains encouraged and confident about the outlook. Outlook * Andrew acknowledged that despite being the largest in the UK, SWC remains small on a global scale. * Very constructive about what can be achieved in 2026 based on all the foundations put in place since IPO. * Andrew thanked shareholders for all their support and kindly asked them to keep giving him their patience and trust based on SWC’s strong delivery record to date. * Reminded people of the "Bitcoin Treasuries Unconference" event set for May 29th - details on the SWC website.

text: Thank you for the update, and congratulations on this huge achievement and all the hard work that went in. What has been accomplished in just nine months is remarkable - not only in terms of execution, but in the strength of the community and spirit that has formed around Smarter Web, its principles, and its mission. As you noted, Smarter Web has delivered on everything it set out to do. That consistency is important because it demonstrates to the market that the team can execute. This, in turn, supports the case for the company to trade at a premium to mNAV: the business has shown it can deliver shareholder value not only through increases in Bitcoin per share, but also by laying incremental building blocks for sustainable future yield, such as the uplisting. Enjoy the weekend, and I’m looking forward to next week. 💪

text: Call notes between @ourgoodlifeuk and @asjwebley . All interpretations are my own. Andrew was very keen to thank all shareholders and stakeholders for their continued support. As outlined in recent weekly updates, 2025 has been a foundational year, focused on putting the right structures in place to enable sustained long-term growth at SWC. Beyond the acquisition of 2,664 Bitcoin, Andrew highlighted his pride in the progress made across: Team build-out, Board composition and other important areas of internal control such as corporate governance The past few months have been challenging due to: 1) share price depreciation and 2) constraints limiting the ability to communicate certain strategic initiatives. Andrew and James reflected on the early days of SWC and the journey to date. When SWC listed on Aquis nine months ago, an LSE listing was not considered feasible due to regulatory views on Bitcoin treasury companies. Initially, the company was required to refer to its holdings as a “digital asset treasury policy” which then evolved into a “Bitcoin treasury strategy policy”, marking meaningful regulatory progress He expressed strong satisfaction with the move to an LSE listing, noting it as proof that the company continues to do what they'll say they do. Andrew believes that, with disciplined execution and responsible growth, FTSE 250 inclusion could be achievable this year. Andrew reiterated the ambition for SWC to become one of the largest companies in the UK if we continue to execute but this will involve volatility along the way. On a personal level, Andrew emphasised that his key focus as CEO is to improve week by week. SWC was discussed as a first mover in the UK, having helped lay the groundwork for peers entering the space. Andrew emphasised the importance of collaboration, noting that “we’re stronger together.” The appointment of new NED (non executive director) Martin Thomas was discussed, highlighting his strong capital markets and legal experience. Andrew also outlined his intention to build a broadly balanced board, noting e.g Randall’s audit and finance expertise. Has desire to add another NED with strong entrepreneurial experience Regarding the uplisting process, Andrew directed shareholders to the relevant RNS. A prospectus will be published (date TBC), which Andrew described as something he is very proud of and which will include comprehensive company information. In line with the company’s 10-year plan, the operating business continues to move in the right direction and remains a core pillar of SWC. Andrew humorously reminded attendees that if anyone needs a website, they should get in touch. Andrew noted that sector sentiment appears to be improving, evidenced by a noticeable increase in inbound activity this year. He acknowledged that sentiment will continue to move in cycles. On the ATM facility, Andrew referred shareholders to the RNS announcement dated 24 December. He expressed satisfaction with the current and anticipated engagement from institutional shareholders. Andrew reiterated the strength and importance of the shareholder community, describing it as a major motivator for him. There may be a raffle for a limited number of tickets to attend the listing day. A shareholder drinks event will be held in London on the evening of the IPO (further details to follow). Andrew and Jesse will be conducting a series of interviews next week.
